original_hypothesis.title: payArtist Permanently Loses ETH on Failed Transfers Due to Unchecked Return Values
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: payArtist zeros collectionTotalAmount before making five ETH transfers via .call(). The success booleans are captured but never checked. If any recipient address is a contract that reverts on ETH receive (or is address(0)), the transfer fails silently. The collectionTotalAmount is already zero, so the failed amount is permanently stuck in the contract with no accounting to reclaim it through payArtist again.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The CEI pattern is partially followed (state zeroed before external calls) but the unchecked returns mean failed transfers result in permanent fund loss rather than atomicity.

proof_of_signal.pattern: collectionTotalAmount[_collectionID] = 0 at line 420; five .call{value} transfers at lines 429-433 with success booleans only used in events

preconditions: At least one recipient address is a contract that reverts on receive, or is address(0)
Admin calls payArtist with approved addresses

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: State zeroed before external calls; call return values unchecked
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No require(success) after any of the five transfers; no revert-on-failure

analysis_questions: Can emergencyWithdraw recover the stuck funds?
What happens if artist proposes a contract address that rejects ETH?
Is there any path to retry failed payments?
validation_requirements: Set artist address to a reverting contract
Call payArtist and verify ETH stuck in contract
Verify collectionTotalAmount is zero preventing retry

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Artist proposes payment addresses including a contract that reverts on ETH receive. Admin approves and calls payArtist. Transfer to that address fails silently. collectionTotalAmount is zeroed. The failed portion is stuck. Only emergencyWithdraw (which drains ALL contract funds) can recover it.
prerequisites: Approved artist addresses include a non-payable contract

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Partial or total loss of collection mint proceeds
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Artists and team members expecting payment
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: emergencyWithdraw can recover funds but drains all collections' balances indiscriminately

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test for failed ETH transfers in payArtist

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: success=false emitted in events but funds irrecoverable via normal path

validation_result.mechanism: unchecked call return value with pre-zeroed accounting
validation_result.impact_summary: If any of the five ETH transfer recipients in payArtist is a contract that reverts on receive, the transfer fails silently. Since collectionTotalAmount is already zeroed, the failed ETH portion is permanently stuck with no retry path except the indiscriminate emergencyWithdraw.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The payArtist function follows a partial CEI pattern: it zeroes collectionTotalAmount[_collectionID] at line ~420 before making five external .call{value:...}("") transfers at lines ~429-433. Each transfer's success boolean is captured but only used in event emissions, never in require() or revert logic. If any recipient address is a contract whose receive/fallback function reverts (or consumes excessive gas), the .call returns success=false without reverting the entire transaction. The ETH intended for that recipient stays in the MinterContract, but collectionTotalAmount is already 0, so calling payArtist again for this collection reverts at the require(collectionTotalAmount[_collectionID] > 0) check. The only recovery mechanism is emergencyWithdraw(), which sends the ENTIRE contract balance to the admin owner — draining funds earmarked for all other collections as well. This is a textbook SWC-104 (Unchecked Call Return Value) vulnerability compounded by the pre-zeroed accounting state.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Lines verified directly in MinterContract.sol source. The collectionTotalAmount zeroing occurs before all five .call transfers. No require/revert on any success boolean. The require(collectionTotalAmount[_collectionID] > 0) guard prevents retry. emergencyWithdraw sends address(this).balance without zeroing any collectionTotalAmount entries.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: collectionTotalAmount is zeroed before transfers. If any .call fails, the ETH remains in the contract but the accounting is already zeroed, preventing retry via payArtist. Success booleans are emitted in events but never checked with require().

calling_sequence: 1. Admin calls payArtist with valid collection ID
7. If primaryAdd1 is a reverting contract, success1 = false, 3 ETH stays in contract
8. Events emitted with success=false but no revert
9. Function completes successfully despite partial transfer failure
10. Subsequent payArtist call reverts: 'Collection Balance must be grater than 0'
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The state mutation (collectionTotalAmount = 0) at step 5 is irrevocable. Steps 6-8 execute without any revert-on-failure mechanism. After step 9, the 3 ETH for primaryAdd1 is stranded in the contract. The only recovery path is emergencyWithdraw() which drains ALL contract ETH to the admin owner, affecting every collection's pending funds.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided that would contradict the vulnerability. The fixturesDeployment.js sets up contracts normally without any special configuration that would prevent this issue.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Scenario: Artist proposes payment addresses via proposePrimaryAddressesAndPercentages, including one address that is a smart contract without a receive/fallback function (or with one that reverts). Admin approves via acceptAddressesAndPercentages. Collection accumulates 10 ETH from mints. Admin calls payArtist. Transfer to the reverting contract fails silently (success=false). collectionTotalAmount is already 0. The failed portion (e.g., 3.3 ETH) is stuck. This can also occur non-maliciously if an artist provides a multisig or smart contract wallet that temporarily cannot receive ETH, or if the artist proposes address(0) which is not validated against. Note: .call to address(0) actually succeeds on most EVM implementations, but any non-payable contract address will cause failure.
validation_result.impact_assessment: Financial impact: Partial or total loss of collection mint proceeds for the affected recipients. The failed transfer amount is permanently stuck unless emergencyWithdraw is called, which indiscriminately drains all contract funds. This means recovering stuck funds for one collection requires draining all other collections' pending payments too, creating a cascading disruption. Affected users: Artists and team members expecting payment from the specific collection.
recommended_mitigations: Add require(success1 && success2 && success3 && success4 && success5) after all transfers to make the function atomic (all-or-nothing)
Alternatively, implement a pull-based payment pattern where recipients withdraw their own funds
If keeping the push pattern, restore collectionTotalAmount on any transfer failure and allow retry
Validate that proposed artist addresses are not address(0) and can receive ETH before approval
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy all contracts using fixturesDeployment.js. 2. Create a collection, set data, costs, phases. 3. Deploy a RevertOnReceive contract (contract with no receive/fallback). 4. As artist, propose primary addresses with one being the RevertOnReceive contract. 5. Admin approves addresses. 6. Mint tokens to accumulate ETH in collectionTotalAmount. 7. Call payArtist. 8. Verify collectionTotalAmount is 0. 9. Verify contract still holds the failed transfer amount. 10. Verify calling payArtist again reverts with 'Collection Balance must be grater than 0'.
uncertainties: The practical likelihood depends on whether artists commonly propose smart contract addresses as payment recipients
A malicious admin could also set _team1 or _team2 to reverting contracts, though this is admin-controlled
Sending ETH to address(0) via .call actually succeeds on most EVM chains, so the address(0) vector may not apply
additional_testing_needed: Test with various failing recipient types: contract without receive(), contract with reverting receive(), contract with high-gas receive()
Test emergencyWithdraw recovery path and verify it correctly recovers stuck funds
Test whether the rounding dust from integer division compounds with failed transfer amounts
